original_abstract:
This paper examines the spatial configuration of the Polish agricultural sector. Utilizing data from the 2010 Agricultural Census, we categorize six dimensions of internal agricultural drivers: land quality and utilization, agrarian structure, socio-demographic indicators, technical modernization, production patterns, and economic output. Together, these factors provide a framework for indexng the general advancement of the sector and evaluating its structural integrity. 

By mapping these spatial matrices against external variables—such as environmental conditions, historical legacies, urbanization, and Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) funding—this study identifies the primary drivers of regional disparity. The findings reveal that natural conditions play only a minor role in shaping contemporary agricultural patterns. Instead, among human-driven factors, historical determinants remain the most influential influence, maintaining a strong presence even in regions with high absorption of European development funds.
humanized_abstract:
The paper focuses on the spatial structure of the Polish agricultural sector. We identified six aspects of the domestic agricultural factors and using data from the Agricultural Census 2010 we discussed them in relation to land use and quality, agricultural structure, socio-demographic characteristics, mechanization of production, types of agricultural activity and agricultural production capacity. We used them to calculate an overall index reflecting the level of advancement of agriculture and to assess its strength and stability.

By relating spatial matrices to relevant non spatial variables such as climatic, historical, socio – economic and Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) the drivers of current disparities are investigated. In the end natural factors appear to have only a marginal impact for the current structure of the agricultural sector, while amongst the variables of human origin, historical determinants have a significant impact even in those regions with the highest degree of absorption of the EU development policies (European agricultural funds).
